interview.strong: Explains that horizontal scaling adds more nodes, while vertical scaling adds more resources to one node.
interview.expert: Contrasts strategies: vertical scaling hits hardware limits and causes restarts, whereas horizontal scaling requires stateless app architecture and load balancing.
interview.leadership: Deploys hybrid scaling platforms that optimize configurations vertically during idle hours and scale horizontally during traffic surges.
interview.coachTips: Connect scaling choices to architecture. Stateless services scale horizontally; databases typically scale vertically.

interview.q: Explain how you would configure rate limiting and circuit breakers for Studio Code Generation & Repository Sync to prevent cascading failures.
interview.intent: Tests reliability patterns, API gateways, service meshes, and network fault tolerance.
interview.hidden: Looks for token bucket algorithms, gateway rate limiting, and service mesh circuit breaking settings.
interview.red: Allowing unlimited traffic loops that crash backend databases.
interview.strong: Proposes setting traffic limits on the gateway and returning error codes when busy.
interview.expert: Configures rate limiting on the API gateway and adds circuit breakers that fail fast when downstream services time out.
interview.leadership: Designs distributed traffic control meshes that dynamically throttle requests during surges, preserving core payment flows.
interview.coachTips: Circuit breakers protect downstream systems. Fail fast to prevent resources from saturating.

interview.q: How do you measure and align SLO availability metrics for Studio Code Generation & Repository Sync with customer SLAs?
interview.intent: Evaluates reliability management, error budget policies, and business alignment.
interview.hidden: Expects rolling SLO calculations, error budget burn rates, and feature-freeze agreements.
interview.red: Treating SLO and SLA as identical, or ignoring error budgets.
interview.strong: Suggests tracking server availability and keeping it above 99.9%.
interview.expert: Calculates SLO availability as a ratio of successful requests. Configures burn-rate alerts that notify the team when budget is spent.
interview.leadership: Enforces automated error budget gates in pipelines that block deployments if availability falls below target thresholds.
interview.coachTips: Error budgets are a policy tool. They resolve the conflict between developer speed and SRE stability.

incident.title: GitHub Actions Nightly Maintenance Pipeline Failure
incident.flow: Nightly cron workflow fails, triggering failure alerts without manual changes.
incident.symptoms: GitHub repository shows failed status badge; alert emails report pipeline execution error.
incident.causes: The workflow script attempts to pull dependencies from external registries that updated their APIs or became temporarily unreachable.
incident.verify: Open GitHub Actions panel and review failed execution logs under the maintenance stage.
incident.fix: Pin dependency versions in package files, and adjust retry settings on network connections.
incident.validation: Manually trigger the workflow runner and confirm all pipeline stages compile and execute.
incident.prevention: Establish local mirror registries caching build packages, reducing external network dependency.

quiz.q: Which file format is utilized to define GitHub Actions workflow pipelines?
quiz.explanation: GitHub Actions workflow pipelines are configured using YAML syntax inside the `.github/workflows` directory.

sre_git_commands.title: Must-Know Git Commands for DevOps Interviews
sre_git_commands.prereqs: Unix CLI, secure shells (SSH/HTTPS), file systems

purpose.solve: Enables systematic version control, codebase alignment, and collaborative change management for large-scale engineering teams.
purpose.why: Created to track revision history, isolate features via branches, resolve merge conflicts, and drive automated CI/CD pipelines.
purpose.before: Manual directory copying (e.g., source_v1, source_final) leading to overwrites, lost code, and zero accountability.
purpose.limits: Large binary file storage (LFS required) and complex branching topologies that can lead to merge debt.
purpose.alternatives: SVN (Apache Subversion), Mercurial, TFS (Team Foundation Server).
purpose.examples: GitHub hosting millions of repositories, SRE teams managing infrastructure-as-code files in monorepos, ArgoCD syncing cluster state directly from Git commits.

sre_git_commands.tips:
Production Value: Git is the interface for GitOps and Infrastructure as Code. Every SRE must know how to safely navigate histories, recover lost commits using reflog, resolve merge conflicts, and optimize repositories using worktrees.

Interactivity Tips:
• Reflog is your safety net: git reflog tracks every single update to references (commits, checkouts, merges). If you accidentially reset or delete a branch, reflog is the only way to recover the dangling commit hashes.
• Rebase vs Merge: Use git rebase to keep a clean, linear history for feature branches. Avoid rebasing shared public branches as it rewrites commit history, causing issues for other collaborators.
• Worktrees for multi-tasking: Instead of stashing or cloning a repo twice to work on two branches simultaneously, use git worktree add to checkout multiple branches in separate directories within the same repository footprint.

flow.learning: git clone ➜ git fetch ➜ git pull ➜ git push ➜ git add ➜ git commit ➜ git stash ➜ git stash pop ➜ git merge ➜ git rebase ➜ git cherry-pick ➜ git reflog ➜ git reset ➜ git revert ➜ git blame ➜ git bisect ➜ git tag ➜ git worktree ➜ git log ➜ git show ➜ git diff
flow.execution: Working Directory (untracked/modified) ➜ Staging Area (indexed via git add) ➜ Local Repository (committed via git commit) ➜ Remote Repository (pushed via git push)

labs.command:
Demo 1 (Feature Dev):
git clone <url> && git checkout -b feature-a && git add . && git commit -m 'feat: feature' && git push origin feature-a

Demo 2 (Linear History):
git checkout main && git pull origin main && git checkout feature-a && git rebase main && git checkout main && git merge feature-a

Demo 3 (Stash & Cherry-Pick):
git stash && git checkout release-1.0 && git cherry-pick <commit-sha> && git checkout feature-a && git stash pop

Demo 4 (Diagnostic Reversion):
git blame -L 10,20 script.js && git bisect start && git bisect bad && git bisect good v1.0.0 && git revert <bad-commit-sha>

Demo 5 (Workspace & Recovery):
git reflog && git reset --hard HEAD@{3} && git tag -a v1.0.1 -m 'v1.0.1 release' && git worktree add ../another-branch-dir another-branch
labs.syntax: git clone [url] || git checkout -b [branch] || git add [path] || git commit -m [msg] || git push [remote] [branch] || git stash || git stash pop || git merge || git rebase || git cherry-pick [sha] || git reflog || git reset --hard [ref] || git revert [sha] || git blame || git bisect || git tag || git worktree || git log || git show || git diff
labs.args:
Demo 1: clone (copies remote repo), checkout -b (creates/switches branch), add (stages modifications), commit -m (commits locally), push (transmits to remote).
Demo 2: pull (fetches and merges), rebase (applies commits on top of base), merge (joins histories).
Demo 3: stash (saves uncommitted changes), cherry-pick (copies target commit SHA), stash pop (restores saved changes).
Demo 4: blame -L (inspects authors for line range), bisect (runs binary search for bugs), revert (applies inverse commit changes).
Demo 5: reflog (shows pointer updates history), reset --hard (forces HEAD and workdir rollback), tag (creates release tag), worktree add (creates concurrent workspace).
labs.output: Output reports showing repository downloads, branch configurations, commit creation states, stash status indexes, conflict warnings, binary search bounds, reflog logs, and worktree creation directories.
labs.fail: Permissions denied during push, merge conflicts during rebase/merge, stash pop file collisions, or non-fast-forward push rejections. Resolve merge conflicts by editing conflict zones, and resolve push blocks by pulling updates first.
labs.prod: Use git push --force-with-lease instead of --force to protect peer commits, set up shallow clones (git clone --depth 1) in automated build containers, and leverage git worktree to work on multiple active branches concurrently without overhead.

archGaps.mermaid:
graph LR
  WD[Working Directory] -- git add --> SA[Staging Area]
  SA -- git commit --> LR[Local Repository]
  LR -- git push --> RR[Remote Repository]
  RR -- git clone / git fetch --> LR
  LR -- git checkout / git reset --> WD
  WD -- git stash --> Stash[(Git Stash)]
  Stash -- git stash pop --> WD
archGaps.spof: Relying on a single remote hosting provider (like GitHub) without mirror repository sync configurations.
archGaps.security: Developers committing secrets (API keys, SSH keys) in cleartext into public repositories, exposing infrastructure access.
archGaps.scaling: Monorepos growing excessively large, slowing down git fetch/pull operations and exceeding disk space limits on build agents.
archGaps.monitoring: No automated repository scanning (e.g. GitGuardian or Trufflehog) monitoring push events for credential leaks.
archGaps.backup: No automated backup of git metadata, pull requests, issue trackers, and release notes outside the main repository host.
archGaps.dr: Failure of remote host services halting continuous deployment pipelines with no secondary source registry failover.
archGaps.improvements: Enforce pre-commit hooks (Trufflehog) to prevent secrets from being committed, enable shallow cloning (git clone --depth 1) in pipelines to scale build speeds, and set up secondary repository mirrors.

git_clone.title: git clone
git_clone.desc: Downloads an existing remote repository onto a local directory, creating remote-tracking branches and downloading all commit history.
git_clone.args: --depth 1: Creates a shallow clone, truncating all historical commits to just the latest revision, optimizing download speeds.
git_clone.output: Cloning into 'portfolio'... Receiving objects: 100%, Resolving deltas: 100%, Done.
git_clone.fail: Authentication failed (invalid SSH keys or personal access tokens), or target repository not found.
git_clone.prod: Enforce --depth 1 in automated build pipelines to save bandwidth and decrease container initialization speeds from minutes to seconds.
git_clone.tips: Cloning copies the entire `.git` history database by default, unlike checkout which just downloads a single snapshot.

git_fetch.title: git fetch
git_fetch.desc: Downloads remote references, branches, and tags from a remote registry to local storage, without merging them into your active branches.
git_fetch.args: --all: Fetch references for all remotes; --prune: Deletes local references to branches that have been deleted in the remote repository.
git_fetch.output: Fetching origin... From github.com:owner/repo * [new branch] dev -> origin/dev - [deleted] obsolete-branch.
git_fetch.fail: Network connection timed out, or remote repository is unreachable.
git_fetch.prod: Run git fetch regularly in automated cron monitors to keep track of upstream branch movements without modifying working code environments.
git_fetch.tips: Fetch is a completely safe command; it only updates remote tracking branches (like origin/main) and never alters your active local branch.

git_pull.title: git pull
git_pull.desc: Fetches upstream updates and immediately integrates them into the active local branch, combining 'git fetch' and 'git merge' in one step.
git_pull.args: --rebase: Reapplies local unpushed commits on top of the remote fetched branch instead of creating a merge commit.
git_pull.fail: Merge conflict errors (concurrent file changes). Git stops and requires editing conflict markers before finalizing the pull.
git_pull.prod: Use `git pull --rebase` by default to avoid cluttering history with empty merge bubbles ('Merge branch main of ...').
git_pull.tips: Configure rebase default: `git config --global pull.rebase true` to automatically rebase when running git pull.

git_push.title: git push
git_push.desc: Publishes local repository branch commits to a remote hosting platform (GitHub/GitLab), updating remote-tracking branch references.
git_push.args: --force-with-lease: A safer force-push that checks if the remote branch has updated since your last fetch before overwriting.
git_push.fail: Rejected non-fast-forward push (remote contains commits you don't have locally). Must pull updates before pushing.
git_push.prod: NEVER run git push --force on shared production branches (main/release). Always prefer --force-with-lease to avoid overwriting coworkers' code.
git_push.tips: Use `git push -u origin <branch>` on first push to track remote branches, simplifying subsequent pushes to just `git push`.

git_add.title: git add
git_add.desc: Stages modifications, files, and deletes in your working directory to the Git staging index area, preparing them for the next commit.
git_add.args: -p: Interactive patch mode. Breaks changes into interactive hunks, letting you review and stage them block-by-block.
git_add.output: Stage this hunk [y,n,q,a,d,j,J,g,/,e,?]? (select y to stage, n to skip).
git_add.fail: Pathspec 'filename' did not match any files (file path is incorrect or file has been deleted).
git_add.prod: Avoid staging blind directories (`git add .`). Use `git status` and `git add -p` to prevent staging secret keys or configuration files.
git_add.tips: The staging area is a virtual index storing a snapshot of changes before they are packed into a commit.

git_commit.title: git commit
git_commit.desc: Saves the staged index snapshot into your local repository commit graph, assigning it a unique SHA-1 hash, author details, and message.
git_commit.command: git commit -m 'feat(auth): add MFA login support'
git_commit.args: -m: Specifies the commit message directly in the CLI, bypassing the system's default text editor.
git_commit.output: [main 4a2b9d3] feat(auth): add MFA login support 2 files changed, 24 insertions(+).
git_commit.fail: Aborting commit due to empty commit message, or nothing staged to commit (run git add first).
git_commit.prod: Enforce Conventional Commits patterns (e.g. feat:, fix:, chore:). Integrate pre-commit hooks to run linting and secret scanning.
git_commit.tips: Write messages in the imperative mood (e.g., 'add feature' instead of 'added feature'), explaining WHAT changed and WHY.

git_stash.title: git stash
git_stash.desc: Temporarily saves your modified working directory files (both tracked and optionally untracked) into a stack, resetting your branch to clean state.
git_stash.args: -u: Includes untracked files in the stash; -m: Labels the stash with a custom description message.
git_stash.output: Saved working directory and index state WIP on feature-a: wip database fix.
git_stash.fail: No local changes detected in working directory to stash.
git_stash.prod: Label stashes clearly. Avoid keeping old stashes. Use `git stash list` to audit and clean old stashed states.
git_stash.tips: A stash is ideal when you need to switch branches quickly to handle a hotfix, without committing half-finished code.

git_stash_pop.title: git stash pop
git_stash_pop.desc: Applies the latest saved stash configuration onto your current working directory and deletes it from the stash stack.
git_stash_pop.args: pops the top stash item (stash@{0}) by default; specify index (e.g. stash@{2}) to pop specific stashes.
git_stash_pop.fail: Merge conflicts when applying stash. Git retains the stash in the stack list so no work is lost.
git_stash_pop.prod: If stash pop triggers conflicts, resolve them immediately and run git stash drop if you verified changes are restored.
git_stash_pop.tips: Use `git stash apply` if you want to restore stashed changes without deleting them from the stash stack.

git_merge.title: git merge
git_merge.desc: Integrates history from one branch into another, creating a merge commit if changes are divergent, or fast-forwarding if linear.
git_merge.args: --no-ff: Forces Git to create a merge commit even if the merge could be resolved as a fast-forward, preserving branch history.
git_merge.output: Merge made by the 'recursive' strategy. script.js | 10 ++.
git_merge.fail: Merge conflicts. Git markers (<<<<<<<) are injected, and the merge halts until conflicts are resolved and committed.
git_merge.prod: Use squash-merges (`git merge --squash`) in pull requests to collapse feature branches into a single clean commit on main.
git_merge.tips: Fast-forward merges occur when target branch HEAD is a direct ancestor of source branch; no merge commit is needed.

git_rebase.title: git rebase
git_rebase.desc: Reapplies local commits from one branch on top of another base branch, rewriting commit hashes to create a clean, linear history.
git_rebase.args: -i: Interactive mode. Opens editor to pick, squash, reword, drop, or fixup commits for the last 3 history points.
git_rebase.fail: Conflicts at commit N. Rebase pauses; resolve conflicts and run `git rebase --continue`, or `git rebase --abort` to cancel.
git_rebase.prod: NEVER rebase branches that have already been pushed to public repositories. You will rewrite history, breaking peer workspaces.
git_rebase.tips: Interactive rebase is an SRE's best tool to cleanup messy WIP local commits before submitting a pull request.

git_cherry_pick.title: git cherry-pick
git_cherry_pick.desc: Applies the changes introduced by an existing commit hash from another branch onto your current active branch HEAD.
git_cherry_pick.args: 4a2b9d3: The specific commit hash to clone and apply as a new commit onto the active branch.
git_cherry_pick.output: [feature-a c8e9f2a] feat(auth): add MFA login support... 2 files changed.
git_cherry_pick.fail: Cherry-pick fails if changes collide with current files, causing conflicts. Resolve and run `git cherry-pick --continue`.
git_cherry_pick.prod: Use cherry-picking to backport surgical hotfixes from development branches to release branches without merging unrelated code.
git_cherry_pick.tips: Cherry-picking copies the changes, creating a new commit with a new SHA hash, even if metadata remains identical.

git_reflog.title: git reflog
git_reflog.desc: Lists all movements of the local HEAD pointer and branch references, recording checkouts, commits, rebase steps, and hard resets.
git_reflog.args: Displays the chronological list of references; HEAD@{0} is the current state, HEAD@{1} is the previous state.
git_reflog.output: 4a2b9d3 HEAD@{0}: reset: moving to HEAD~1; c8e9f2a HEAD@{1}: commit: add test.
git_reflog.fail: Reflog only exists locally; it is not pushed to remote repositories and cannot be queried for remote servers.
git_reflog.prod: Reflog is an SRE's emergency recovery tool. If you accidentally run `git reset --hard` or delete a local branch, use reflog to find the commit hash.
git_reflog.tips: Dangling commits (not referenced by any branch/tag) are kept in local database for about 30 days before being garbage collected.

git_reset.title: git reset
git_reset.desc: Resets the current HEAD to a specified state, optionally updating the staging index and working directory.
git_reset.args: --hard: Resets HEAD, the staging area, AND discards all uncommitted modifications in the working directory.
git_reset.output: HEAD is now at c8e9f2a feat: verify configurations.
git_reset.fail: Fails if target commit SHA or shorthand pointer is invalid.
git_reset.prod: Avoid git reset --hard on dirty trees. You will lose all uncommitted changes permanently. Use --soft to keep files.
git_reset.tips: --soft keeps changes in staging; --mixed (default) keeps changes in working directory but unstaged; --hard discards all changes.

git_revert.title: git revert
git_revert.desc: Creates a new commit that applies the exact inverse changes of a target commit hash, safely reversing history without rewriting it.
git_revert.args: 4a2b9d3: The commit SHA containing changes you want to safely undo.
git_revert.output: [feature-a e2d3c4b] Revert 'feat(auth): add MFA login support'... 2 files changed.
git_revert.fail: Fails with conflicts if subsequent commits modified the same lines. Resolve and commit the revert.
git_revert.prod: Use `git revert` for shared public branches (main/release). It keeps history linear and doesn't break peer repositories.
git_revert.tips: Because revert creates a new commit, it is the safest and most accountable way to rollback changes in production.

git_blame.title: git blame
git_blame.desc: Annotates each line in a file with the commit hash, author name, and timestamp of the last revision that modified that line.
git_blame.args: -L 45,55: Restricts annotation to lines 45 through 55 of the target script.js file.
git_blame.fail: File path does not exist, or line numbers exceed file bounds.
git_blame.prod: Use blame to find the exact commit that introduced a line of code, helping trace context during root cause analysis.
git_blame.tips: Use `-w` flag to ignore whitespace changes when running blame to see real code modifications.

git_bisect.title: git bisect
git_bisect.desc: Uses binary search to find the specific commit that introduced a bug or regression in your repository history.
git_bisect.command: git bisect start && git bisect bad && git bisect good v1.0.0
git_bisect.args: start: Begins bisect; bad: Marks current revision as buggy; good v1.0.0: Marks v1.0.0 release tag as clean.
git_bisect.output: Bisecting: 15 commits left to test after this (approx 4 steps). [commit-sha].
git_bisect.fail: Invalid refs, or conflicting history graphs preventing binary traversal paths.
git_bisect.prod: Automate debugging using `git bisect run npm test`. Git will run the test script on each bisect node automatically until it finds the culprit.
git_bisect.tips: Bisecting cuts debugging times exponentially, reducing search space from hundreds of commits to just log2(N) steps.

git_tag.title: git tag
git_tag.desc: Creates reference pointers to specific points in history, typically used to mark release versions (e.g. v1.0.0) following semantic versioning.
git_tag.args: -a: Creates an annotated tag containing author, date, and message; -m: Declares the description message.
git_tag.fail: Tag name already exists (must delete or use force flag -f).
git_tag.prod: Always use annotated tags (-a) for releases. Push them to remote servers using `git push origin v1.0.2` or `--tags`.
git_tag.tips: Lightweight tags are just refs pointing to commits. Annotated tags are full objects in the Git database.

git_worktree.title: git worktree
git_worktree.desc: Manages multiple concurrent working directories (worktrees) linked to a single Git repository database, allowing you to checkout multiple branches simultaneously.
git_worktree.args: add ../hotfix-dir hotfix-branch: Checks out hotfix-branch into a new directory sibling to the current repository.
git_worktree.output: Preparing worktree (checking out 'hotfix-branch'). HEAD is now at 4a2b9d3.
git_worktree.fail: Branch is already checked out in another worktree (Git prohibits checking out the same active branch twice).
git_worktree.prod: Use worktrees to switch to production hotfixes without stashing, discarding, or double-cloning large codebases.
git_worktree.tips: When done, remove the worktree using `git worktree prune` or `git worktree remove <path>` to clean up directory mappings.

git_log.title: git log
git_log.desc: Displays the commit history logs for active branches, showing authors, hashes, dates, and commit messages.
git_log.args: --oneline: Compresses commit info; --graph: Renders commit tree links; --decorate: Shows branch tags; -n 5: Limit to latest 5 entries.
git_log.output:
* 4a2b9d3 (HEAD -> feature-a, origin/feature-a) feat: auth
* c8e9f2a merge main
git_log.prod: Use custom git log alias commands in local profiles to quickly audit commit relationships and check linear release structures.
git_log.tips: Use filters like `--author='Name'` or `--grep='fix'` to search history for specific authors or commit messages.

git_show.title: git show
git_show.desc: Displays detailed metadata and code difference content changes for a specific commit hash, reference, or file snapshot.
git_show.args: 4a2b9d3:script.js: Extracts and displays the raw content of script.js as it existed at the moment commit 4a2b9d3 was created.
git_show.output: Renders the full file code layout of script.js at that commit hash snapshot.
git_show.fail: Commit hash does not exist, or path is incorrect.
git_show.prod: Use `git show` to verify exact code payloads pushed in deployment failure alerts, inspecting lines without modifying local working copy.
git_show.tips: Running `git show <commit>` shows both commit details (author, date) and the diff patch of all files modified in that commit.

git_diff.title: git diff
git_diff.desc: Displays differences in file lines between your working directory, staging index, commits, or branches.
git_diff.args: --staged (or --cached): Renders differences between files in the staging area and the latest commit snapshot.
git_diff.output: Diff comparison lines showing deleted lines in red (-) and added lines in green (+).
git_diff.fail: Target file not modified or untracked.
git_diff.prod: Always run `git diff` or `git diff --staged` before running `git commit` to verify you aren't committing temporary print statements or debug flags.
git_diff.tips: Use `git diff branch-a..branch-b` to inspect differences between two distinct branches before merging.

interview.q: Explain the differences between git merge and git rebase, when you would use each, and how git stash and git cherry-pick fit into daily workflows.
interview.intent: Assesses candidate's command over Git histories, features isolation, and advanced commit manipulation.
interview.hidden: Interviewer wants to know about keeping a clean, linear commit history, avoiding rewriting shared history, and retrieving individual commits.
interview.red: Rebasing shared remote branches (like main), or failing to explain how cherry-pick extracts specific commits by hash.
interview.strong: Uses merge to preserve history chronology and merge commits. Uses rebase on local feature branches to maintain a clean linear commit graph. Uses stash to temporarily save uncommitted changes, and cherry-pick to apply a specific commit from one branch to another.
interview.expert: Explains that merge creates a new merge commit (non-destructive history), while rebase applies local commits sequentially on top of the target base branch (destructively rewriting commit IDs). Explains git stash saves dirty state in ref/stash, and cherry-pick copies commit payloads onto HEAD, generating a new commit ID.
interview.leadership: Establishes organization-wide branching guidelines, enforcing squash-merge rules on main branches to ensure clear release tags while maintaining rebase requirements on active feature development.
interview.coachTips: Explain how rebase flattens the commit graph. Make sure to call out the golden rule: never rebase public shared branches.

interview.q: If you accidentally perform a hard reset (git reset --hard) and lose uncommitted/committed code, how do you recover? How does git revert differ from git reset?
interview.intent: Tests the candidate's crisis management skills when recovering lost code and their understanding of modifying local vs remote histories.
interview.hidden: Looks for understanding of git reflog to find dangling commit hashes, and when to use safe reverts (non-destructive) vs resets (rewriting history).
interview.red: Assuming that a git reset --hard is completely unrecoverable, or using git reset to modify pushed remote commits on shared branches.
interview.strong: Uses git reflog to locate the commit hash before the reset, then runs git checkout or git reset to recover it. Explains git reset moves the HEAD pointer backward, while git revert creates a new commit that applies inverse changes.
interview.expert: Details running 'git reflog' to inspect local reference movements, finding the dangling commit SHA, and running 'git reset --hard HEAD@{index}' to restore files. Explains that git revert is safe for public branches because it doesn't alter previous commits, whereas git reset rewrites history and requires force pushing (git push --force-with-lease) which breaks collaborators' local clones.
interview.leadership: Enforces commit sig-verification and disables force-push permissions on main branches using repository rulesets, preventing accidental history overrides while establishing automated backups for commit graphs.
interview.coachTips: Highlight git reflog. It's the ultimate undo history. Explain that Git doesn't garbage collect dangling commits immediately, giving you a ~30-day safety net.

interview.q: How do you locate the commit that introduced a bug in a codebase with hundreds of commits? What is git worktree and how does it optimize developer operations?
interview.intent: Verifies advanced diagnostic capability using binary searches, and resource optimization during multi-branch development.
interview.hidden: Interviewer wants details on git bisect automation (bisect run), git blame for target lines, and checking out multiple paths without double cloning.
interview.red: Checking out commits manually in a sequential loop to locate a bug, or cloning a repository multiple times to work on different branches simultaneously.
interview.strong: Uses git bisect to perform a binary search between a known good and bad commit. Uses git blame to find who modified specific lines. Uses git worktree to manage multiple active working directories from a single local repo.
interview.expert: Explains git bisect start ➜ git bisect bad ➜ git bisect good. Details how 'git bisect run test-script.sh' automates the search via exit codes. Explains 'git worktree add ../feature-dir feat-branch' links separate working trees to a single .git database, saving storage and context-switching overhead.
interview.leadership: Integrates git bisect scripts within automated testing pipelines to identify regression points, creating standardized worktree automation templates for local development environments.
interview.coachTips: Mention that git bisect run can automate debugging by running a unit test script. Emphasize how worktrees allow debugging without dirtying active workspaces.

interview.q: What is the difference between git fetch and git pull? How do you inspect differences between branches or specific commits using git diff and git show?
interview.intent: Tests basic repository synchronization mechanics and file difference analysis.
interview.hidden: Wants to hear about pull being fetch + merge, checking remote updates without merging, and filtering diff results by file.
interview.red: Running git pull blindly without understanding it can trigger unwanted merge commits, or confusing git diff with git log.
interview.strong: Explains git fetch downloads remote tracking branches without changing local HEAD, while git pull fetches and merges. Uses git diff to see code changes and git show to inspect metadata and contents of a specific commit.
interview.expert: Explains git pull runs git fetch and then git merge under the hood (or git rebase if configured). Details git diff options like --stat, comparing working directory to index, and using 'git show <commit-sha>:<file-path>' to view a file at a specific historical point.
interview.leadership: Establishes developer sync schedules, configuring git pull --rebase as the organization default to prevent pull merge-bubbles, and integrating automated repository status checks in IDE modules.
interview.coachTips: A common pitfall is not understanding that pull is fetch + merge. Explain why pull --rebase is safer to prevent merge-bubble commits.

interview.q: Walk through the complete lifecycle of creating, staging, committing, tagging, and pushing a feature from local sandbox to production branch. What are the best practices for commit message formats?
interview.intent: Verifies developer hygiene, understanding of git tracking states, and semantic version tagging.
interview.hidden: Expects detailing of untracked ➜ staged ➜ local commit ➜ annotated tag ➜ remote push with tags. Wants discussion of Conventional Commits format.
interview.red: Committing directly to main, staging files blindly using git add . without verifying contents, or not tagging production releases.
interview.strong: Uses git add to stage, git commit to record locally, git tag to mark version, and git push to publish. Follows standard commit formats (e.g. feat:, fix:).
interview.expert: Details staging specific hunks using 'git add -p', writing commits following Conventional Commits format (type(scope): message), creating an annotated tag (git tag -a v1.0.0 -m 'message'), and pushing with '--follow-tags' to remote hosts.
interview.leadership: Automates release notes generation and semantic versioning triggers (such as semantic-release) driven entirely by Conventional Commits in merged pull requests.
interview.coachTips: Use Conventional Commits syntax (e.g. feat:, fix:) to showcase professional code hygiene. Highlight annotated tags (-a) as official release boundaries.

incident.title: Merge Conflict Blocking Production Hotfix Deployment
incident.flow: Release branch pull request blocks merge due to conflicting line changes in tools scripts.
incident.symptoms: CI pipeline fails at merge stage; pull request UI displays 'This branch has conflicts that must be resolved'.
incident.causes: Two developers modified the same code lines in script.js concurrently without syncing changes.
incident.verify: git checkout production && git merge hotfix-branch
incident.fix: Manually edit conflicting files to resolve HEAD indicators (<<<<<<< to >>>>>>>), stage changes (git add), and finalize commit.
incident.validation: Verify file compiles correctly, run npm run build, and verify git status shows clean working directory.
incident.prevention: Enforce frequent pulls from main branch, run small pull requests, and leverage git rebase regularly to resolve conflicts early.

quiz.q: Which command allows you to view the log of all local HEAD pointer movements, including checked out branches and hard resets?
quiz.explanation: git reflog records every update to reference tips (HEAD, branches, tags) in the local repository, making it easy to recover deleted commits or undo resets.

quiz.q: How does 'git checkout' differ from 'git worktree' when switching context between branches?
options: checkout moves HEAD in the current directory; worktree creates a new directory to work on another branch concurrently
checkout deletes uncommitted changes; worktree stashes them
worktree can only be used on remote servers
quiz.a: checkout moves HEAD in the current directory; worktree creates a new directory to work on another branch concurrently
quiz.explanation: git worktree creates separate physical directories linked to a single local repository, allowing you to checkout and run multiple branches in parallel without cloning the repo multiple times.

quiz.q: What is the main danger of running 'git push --force' instead of 'git push --force-with-lease'?
options: It overwrites any commits made on the remote branch by other developers since your last pull
It changes local SSH configuration keys
quiz.a: It overwrites any commits made on the remote branch by other developers since your last pull
quiz.explanation: --force-with-lease checks if the remote branch has updated since your last local fetch, preventing you from accidentally overwriting changes pushed by other team members.

quiz.q: Which Git command applies the changes introduced by an existing commit hash from another branch onto your current HEAD?
quiz.explanation: git cherry-pick copies the changes from a specific commit hash and applies them as a new commit on the active branch HEAD.

quiz.q: What algorithm does 'git bisect' utilize to locate the commit that introduced a bug or regression?
quiz.explanation: git bisect performs a binary search through the repository's commit history between a known good and bad commit to quickly isolate the breaking change.

sre_devops_roadmap.title: SRE & DevOps Toolchain Integration Path
sre_devops_roadmap.prereqs: Unix CLI, system virtualization, cloud-native architecture principles
sre_devops_roadmap.advanced: GitOps Automation, Multi-Cloud Orchestration, Service Mesh Fabric

purpose.solve: Unifies disconnected SRE and DevOps engineering tools into a single, cohesive deployment pipeline and feedback loop.
purpose.why: Created to systematically bridge code control, virtualization, infrastructure provisioning, automated releases, and observability.
purpose.before: Siloed configurations where developers managed code, sysadmins manually set up servers, and operators checked logs separately.
purpose.limits: Managing distinct tool integration domains adds operational overhead, requiring strict policy enforcement and configuration standards.
purpose.alternatives: Fully integrated cloud proprietary pipelines (e.g. AWS CodePipeline, Azure DevOps) or raw manual scripts.
purpose.examples: High-throughput SaaS companies deploying microservices using Terraform, ArgoCD, and Istio with Prometheus observability.

sre_devops_roadmap.tips:
Production Value: Modern DevOps is about toolchain integration, not just running isolated commands. When a service goes down, you must trace issues from the Git commit down to the Linux container, Kubernetes routing, and Istio meshes using Grafana dashboards.

Key Roadmap Integrations:
• Infrastructure-as-Code: Use Terraform to define cloud infrastructure, then use Helm to provision software inside Kubernetes namespaces.
• Continuous Deployment: Sync Git changes to Kubernetes clusters using ArgoCD GitOps controllers, bypassing manual kubectl deployments.
• Observability: Collect metrics using Prometheus agents, visualize them via Grafana dashboards, and route alerts dynamically.

flow.learning: Git ➜ Linux ➜ Docker ➜ Kubernetes ➜ Helm ➜ Terraform ➜ AWS ➜ Jenkins ➜ ArgoCD ➜ Prometheus ➜ Grafana ➜ Service Mesh (Istio) ➜ SRE Concepts
flow.execution: Git Commit ➜ Jenkins Pipeline ➜ Docker Build ➜ Terraform Plan/Apply ➜ Helm Chart Deployment ➜ ArgoCD Sync ➜ Ingress Gateway Routing ➜ Envoy Sidecar Redirect ➜ Prometheus Metrics Scrape ➜ Grafana Telemetry Visualization

labs.command: git status && docker build -t app:v1 . && terraform plan && helm install app-chart ./charts
labs.syntax: git [cmd] || docker build -t [tag] [dir] || terraform plan || helm install [release] [chart]
labs.args: plan: Previews infrastructure changes in Terraform; install: Deploys a Helm chart release onto the target Kubernetes cluster.
labs.output: Terminal logs showing staged Git changes, compiled Docker layers, Terraform plan resources, and Helm release deployment metadata.
labs.fail: Docker daemon unreachable, Terraform credentials expired, or Helm release name collision. Remediate by validating local contexts.
labs.prod: Run Terraform in automated CI runners using remote state locks (e.g. AWS S3/DynamoDB) and automate Helm promotions using ArgoCD GitOps.

archGaps.mermaid:
graph TD
  Git[Git Commit] -- Trigger --> CI[Jenkins CI Pipeline]
  CI -- Build & Push --> Registry[Docker Image Registry]
  CI -- Provision --> TF[Terraform IaC]
  TF -- Deploy Infra --> AWS[AWS Cloud]
  Registry -- Pull Image --> K8s[Kubernetes Pods]
  K8s -- Sync --> CD[ArgoCD GitOps]
  AWS -- Host --> K8s
  K8s -- Expose Metrics --> Prom[Prometheus Scraper]
  Prom -- Visualize --> Grafana[Grafana Dashboards]
  K8s -- Intercept Traffic --> Mesh[Istio Envoy Mesh]
archGaps.spof: Relying on a single Jenkins master node for build scheduling, creating a critical automation pipeline single point of failure.
archGaps.security: Plaintext secrets checked into Git or passed unencrypted between Terraform state files and Jenkins build nodes.
archGaps.scaling: Monolithic Jenkins builds queuing due to limited runner resources and lack of autoscaling Kubernetes executor agents.
archGaps.monitoring: Alert fatigue from poorly configured alerting thresholds in Prometheus, leading to ignored notifications.
archGaps.backup: Terraform state locks not backed up, risking database corruption and loss of resource state tracking.
archGaps.dr: No cross-region failover strategy for the ArgoCD control plane, causing configuration lockouts during regional cloud outages.
archGaps.improvements: Transition Jenkins builds to Kubernetes dynamic pod executors, encrypt Terraform states via KMS, and deploy highly available ArgoCD clusters across multiple availability zones.

git.title: Git
git.desc: Version control system tracking codebase changes, isolating work via branches, and initiating CI/CD automation pipelines.
git.args: -n 5: Displays only the last 5 commits in the branch history logs to keep summaries concise.
git.output: Staged/unstaged files, and chronological branch history hashes.
git.fail: Not a git repository error (run git init first).
git.prod: Use Conventional Commits syntax to enable automated release notes generation inside CI workflows.
git.tips: Git is the absolute entry point of SRE. All infrastructure modifications must start with a commit.

linux.title: Linux
linux.desc: The foundational operating system of cloud containers, node servers, and orchestration systems. Powers kernel-level namespaces and control groups.
linux.args: -u docker.service: Restricts system logs to Docker daemon events; -n 50: Limit output to 50 lines.
linux.output: Daemon boot notifications, socket activation events, and container lifecycle logs.
linux.fail: Permission denied (requires sudo privilege to read systemd journals).
linux.prod: Optimize kernel parameters like file descriptors (fs.file-max) and socket queue limits under /etc/sysctl.conf to scale high-concurrency systems.
linux.tips: Understanding CPU schedulers, memory managers, and syslogs is mandatory to diagnose container crashes (OOMKilled).

docker.title: Docker
docker.desc: Lightweight OS-level virtualization platform packing applications and dependencies into isolated container namespaces.
docker.args: --format: Extracts specific JSON state parameters like health checks, process IDs, and exit status code fields.
docker.output: JSON block mapping execution status, network bindings, and system mounts.
docker.fail: Container 'app-container' not found or Docker daemon is offline.
docker.prod: Always use multi-stage Dockerfiles and minimal base images (e.g. alpine or distroless) to optimize image sizes and minimize security vulnerabilities.
docker.tips: Docker isolation relies on Linux Namespaces (network, mount, pid) and Cgroups (resource limits: CPU/memory).

kubernetes.title: Kubernetes
kubernetes.desc: Open-source container orchestration engine managing container scaling, scheduling, self-healing, and networking overlays.
kubernetes.args: -l app=web: Targets pods matching selector labels, displaying status, events, and lifecycle limits.
kubernetes.output: Pod telemetry detail including scheduling logs, volumes, readiness status, and lifecycle events.
kubernetes.fail: API connection timeout or target pods not found in namespace.
kubernetes.prod: Never run pods without resource requests/limits configured. This prevents single pods from monopolizing host memory.
kubernetes.tips: Kubernetes is a declarative control plane. It continuously resolves the drift between actual state and target state.

helm.title: Helm
helm.desc: The package manager for Kubernetes. Packages YAML manifests into parameterized, reusable structures called charts.
helm.args: -n production: Lists all deployed Helm chart releases in the production namespace.
helm.output: Chart release names, revision numbers, updates timestamp, status, and app version numbers.
helm.fail: Namespace not found, or chart inventory database unreachable.
helm.prod: Store Helm values configurations in Git (GitOps) and run helm lint and dry-runs inside CI verification steps.
helm.tips: Helm handles templating. It makes Kubernetes deployments reusable across Dev, Staging, and Production environments.

terraform.title: Terraform
terraform.desc: Infrastructure as Code tool managing cloud platforms securely. Translates human-readable configuration files into API calls.
terraform.args: -out=tfplan: Saves the calculated execution plan safely, ensuring the exact same plan is applied during promotion.
terraform.output: Summary detailing additions, modifications, and deletions of cloud assets.
terraform.fail: Terraform lock file active, credentials invalid, or syntax errors in tf files.
terraform.prod: Use remote state backends (like AWS S3) with state locking (DynamoDB) to prevent concurrent executions from corrupting infrastructure state.
terraform.tips: Terraform is declarative. You declare what resource you want, and Terraform calculates the DAG (Direct Acyclic Graph) to create it.

aws.title: AWS
aws.desc: The underlying public cloud platform hosting compute instances, database backends, virtual private networks, and managed services.
aws.args: --filters: Restricts description to active, running compute nodes in the active region.
aws.output: JSON block detailing instance IDs, public/private IPs, VPC IDs, and security configurations.
aws.fail: AWS credentials expired or region configuration missing.
aws.prod: Configure IAM policies following the principle of least privilege, mapping role credentials dynamically to Kubernetes ServiceAccounts (IRSA).
aws.tips: In AWS SRE, manage all infrastructure via API. Treat servers as cattle, not pets: design for failure across multiple Availability Zones.

jenkins.title: Jenkins
jenkins.desc: Automation server orchestration tool driving CI/CD pipelines, executing automated build tasks, security audits, and code testing steps.
jenkins.args: -u admin:token: Auths API trigger requests using secure authentication tokens to start build pipelines.
jenkins.output: HTTP status 201 Created indicating the build has been successfully queued.
jenkins.fail: Authentication denied (invalid token) or endpoint unreachable.
jenkins.prod: Use Declarative Pipelines defined in Jenkinsfile stored in Git. Avoid configuring jobs via Jenkins GUI to maintain pipeline-as-code standards.
jenkins.tips: Jenkins runs agent nodes. Distribute workloads across dynamic ephemeral agents running as containers inside Kubernetes.

argocd.title: ArgoCD
argocd.desc: Declarative GitOps continuous delivery tool for Kubernetes. Synchronizes application configurations from Git to live clusters.
argocd.args: production-app: Commands the ArgoCD controller to immediately pull Git updates and sync live cluster resources.
argocd.output: Sync execution logs detailing target states, endpoints, and health status indicators.
argocd.fail: ArgoCD API server unreachable or authentication token expired.
argocd.prod: Enable auto-sync with self-healing options. This automatically reverts any manual edits (drift) made on the cluster to match Git.
argocd.tips: GitOps rules: Git is the single source of truth. Manual modifications to live environments are strictly prohibited.

prometheus.title: Prometheus
prometheus.desc: Time-series monitoring database. Scrapes metric endpoints from container systems, recording metrics data to drive alerting pathways.
prometheus.args: /api/v1/targets: Queries the Prometheus server to verify metric scraper state loops across nodes.
prometheus.output: Active scrapers status, target IPs, metrics endpoints, scrape intervals, and scrape duration.
prometheus.prod: Use Alertmanager to group metrics alerts, deduplicate alerts during incidents, and route notifications to Slack/PagerDuty.
prometheus.tips: Prometheus uses pull-based monitoring (scraping metrics endpoints) and queries metrics using PromQL.

grafana.title: Grafana
grafana.desc: Visualization platform connecting time-series databases. Builds clean, interactive dashboards mapping live infrastructure health metrics.
grafana.args: Bearer api-key: Authenticates API requests to fetch dashboard configuration JSON blocks.
grafana.output: JSON payload representing dashboard panel grids, queries, variables, and styling configurations.
grafana.fail: Grafana API key invalid or dashboard UID not found.
grafana.prod: Write Grafana dashboards as code (JSON/Terraform) to ensure dashboards can be version-controlled and restored instantly.
grafana.tips: Grafana unifies multi-source metrics, logs, and trace spans into singular, readable dashboards.

service_mesh.title: Service Mesh (Istio)
service_mesh.desc: The infrastructure layer managing microservice network communications, injecting secure mTLS tunnels, routing traffic, and generating traces.
service_mesh.args: pod-name.default: Audits endpoint cluster routing tables on the Envoy proxy sidecar container of the target pod.
service_mesh.output: IP endpoints, routing weights, port allocations, and service targets configuration mappings.
service_mesh.fail: Pod does not contain Istio sidecar or Istio control plane is down.
service_mesh.prod: Transition to sidecarless Ambient meshes (e.g. Istio Ambient) to save memory usage and reduce container network overhead.
service_mesh.tips: Mesh separates network control. Developers write code, and the mesh handles retries, timeouts, and secure communication tunnels.

sre_concepts.title: SRE Concepts
sre_concepts.desc: Foundational operations framework ensuring system reliability. Governed by SLOs, SLAs, SLIs, Error Budgets, and Blameless Postmortems.
sre_concepts.args: rules.yml: Validates PromQL alert logic, checking if SLA threshold triggers are configured correctly.
sre_concepts.fail: Syntax error in PromQL query expressions or incorrect YAML formatting.
sre_concepts.prod: Enforce Error Budgets: if the budget is exhausted, halt feature development and pivot all engineering focus onto reliability tasks.
sre_concepts.tips: SRE is not just about tools. It's about engineering incentives: balancing velocity (DevOps) against reliability (SRE).

interview.q: How do you design a complete CI/CD GitOps pipeline from code commit to Kubernetes deployment, and how does the toolchain fit together?
interview.intent: Tests structural integration knowledge across SRE/DevOps lifecycle tools.
interview.hidden: Wants to see how git commits flow through build registries, deployment IaC, Kubernetes clusters, and GitOps control loops.
interview.red: Confusing CI (building images) with CD (deploying state), or proposing manually modifying kubernetes manifests at deployment stages.
interview.strong: Describes code pushed to Git triggering Jenkins, building a Docker image, updating Helm manifests, and using ArgoCD to sync the manifests to Kubernetes.
interview.expert: Details complete path: Git commit triggers Webhook to Jenkins ➜ Jenkins builds and pushes versioned Docker image to ECR ➜ Jenkins commits updated image tag to Helm values file in Git ➜ ArgoCD controller detects Git state changes and applies manifests to Kubernetes ➜ Ingress Gateway routes traffic dynamically.
interview.leadership: Architects a zero-trust automated pipeline where IaC (Terraform) provisions base clusters, ArgoCD deploys Helm configurations dynamically, and policy controllers (OPA/Kyverno) audit manifests in Git before sync runs occur.
interview.coachTips: Explain the separation of concerns: CI builds state, Git holds configuration state, and CD reconciles cluster state.

interview.q: Walk through a full troubleshooting scenario where an application is experiencing 5xx errors, explaining which tools you check at each layer.
interview.intent: Evaluates systemic diagnostic capabilities across the SRE toolchain from edge to kernel.
interview.hidden: Wants to see structured layer-by-layer traversal (Grafana dashboards ➜ Prometheus alerts ➜ Ingress routing ➜ Pod logs ➜ Linux kernel/kubelet).
interview.red: Assuming the bug is in a random module without checking metrics first, or starting with destructive actions like server restarts.
interview.strong: Checks Grafana dashboards to identify traffic spikes, Prometheus logs to find when alerts fired, and kubectl logs to inspect pod failures.
interview.expert:
Traces the issue systematically:
1. Check Grafana to identify target services returning 502/504 errors.
2. Run PromQL queries to check connection pools and CPU stats.
3. Inspect Envoy proxy config mesh using istioctl.
4. Run kubectl describe pod to find events/OOM kills.
5. Check journalctl logs on the host to verify kernel-level events.
interview.leadership: Establishes end-to-end trace span collection using OpenTelemetry, pushing traces to centralized Jaeger databases to map exact microservice failure blocks automatically.
interview.coachTips: Never jump to conclusions. Start broad with visualization (Grafana), drill down to query metrics (Prometheus), and surgical diagnostics (kubectl, journalctl).

interview.q: What are SLIs, SLOs, and SLAs, and how do you implement error budget policies to align developer and SRE team goals?
interview.intent: Tests understanding of SRE reliability management frameworks and engineering team incentives.
interview.hidden: Expects clear mathematical distinction between SLIs, SLOs, and SLAs, and real-world application of error budget exhaustion policies.
interview.red: Treating SLA and SLO as identical concepts, or treating error budget exhaustion as a passive indicator with no enforcement.
interview.strong: Defines SLI as a service metric (e.g. success rate), SLO as the target reliability goal (e.g. 99.9%), and SLA as the legal contract with customers. Explains that error budget is 100% minus the SLO.
interview.expert: Explains SLI (numerator/denominator metrics), SLO (target value over rolling 30-day window), and SLA (includes legal/financial penalties). Details error budget enforcement policies: if budget is exhausted, block new feature pushes, route developers to fix reliability issues, and redirect alert thresholds.
interview.leadership: Designs organization-wide SRE dashboard metrics dynamically derived from live Prometheus telemetry, enforcing automated deployment gate locks when service error budgets drop below minimum limits.
interview.coachTips: Emphasize that the error budget is a shared tool: it gives developers permission to fail and take risks, up to the SLO boundary.
